# Best digital intake forms therapists can start using today You know the scene: a new client arrives five minutes before the hour, you hand over a clipboard, and the next fifteen minutes are lost to scribbling while you watch the clock eat into session time. Then comes the scanning, the re-typing, the “I can’t read this phone number” dance. Digital intake forms won’t fix everything, but they will give you back that quarter-hour, keep you compliant, and stop the paper pile from breeding on your desk. In this post I walk you through the two tools I actually tested in my own solo practice—SimplePractice and Jotform—so you can decide which one fits your workflow without wasting an evening on demos. > **Quick verdict:** If you want an EHR that already includes scheduling, notes, billing, and intake, SimplePractice is the no-brainer. If you already have an EHR and just need cheap, flexible forms you can brand yourself, Jotform wins on price and design freedom. ## SimplePractice – best all-in-one EHR with built-in intake SimplePractice folds intake into the same subscription you already pay for scheduling and notes. The price is $39–$59 per month depending on whether you need the basic or plus tier; there is no extra charge for digital forms. Inside the client portal you get 15 pre-built intake templates—PHQ-9, GAD-7, informed consent, credit-card auth, minors, couples—mapped directly to the client record so answers auto-populate the header of every progress note. Clients receive a link when they book; they can finish the packet on any phone without downloading an app, and you get a green check-mark in the calendar once everything is signed. Encryption is HIPAA, GDPR, and PIPEDA compliant, and the Business Associate Agreement is included. If you practice in the US, UK, AU, or CA, your data stays in regional data centres, and you can process card payments without a third-party gateway. The mobile app for clinicians lets you see whether intake is pending while you’re still at the grocery store on Sunday afternoon. Storage on the lowest tier is capped at 1 GB, which is enough for around 400 scanned insurance cards; if you upgrade to the plus plan you get unlimited storage. The portal is not white-label, so tech-savvy clients will see SimplePractice branding, and you cannot add conditional logic beyond show/hide sections. Support is live seven days a week, which came in handy when I botched the consent language on a Friday night. ## Jotform – best flexible builder for custom therapy packets Jotform started life as a generic form builder, but the company now offers a HIPAA plan at $34 per month that includes a signed BAA, 256-bit encryption, and your choice of US, EU, or AU data centres—important if you live outside the United States. The free tier gives you five forms, but you will need the paid plan for therapy work because of the compliance requirement. There are over 900 healthcare widgets: e-signature, photo-ID upload, file download, payment collection, and even a Spanish-language PHQ-9 that toggles automatically if the client selects Español. Conditional logic is drag-and-drop; for example, you can hide trauma history questions unless the client indicates prior therapy, or display a credit-card field only when copay is greater than zero. Branding is fully white-label, so you can park the form at intake.yourpractice.com and drop in your logo, colours, and fonts. Integrations are where Jotform shines: you can push responses to Google Drive, Dropbox, or via Zapier into SimplePractice, TherapyNotes, or TheraNest. The downside is that Jotform is not an EHR, so you will still need to export PDFs or pay for a Zapier subscription ($20 per month) to auto-import client data. Support is Monday to Friday, which once delayed a fix when my trauma screening logic broke on a Saturday morning. Storage on the HIPAA plan is 1 TB, roughly enough for 200,000 intake packets with insurance-card photos. If you already have an EHR you love and just want the cheapest compliant way to collect custom packets, Jotform is hard to beat. ## Head-to-head comparison table | Feature | SimplePractice | Jotform | |---|---|---| | Monthly cost | $39–$59 flat | $0–$34 + Zapier if needed | | HIPAA BAA | Included on all paid tiers | Included on HIPAA plan | | Therapy-specific templates | 15+ pre-built | Generic, but editable | | Conditional logic | Basic show/hide | Advanced drag-and-drop | | White-label portal | No | Yes | | Data centres | US, UK, AU, CA | US, EU, AU selectable | | Storage | 1 GB–unlimited | 100 GB–1 TB | | Live support | 7 days | Monday–Friday | ## Who should choose which Choose SimplePractice if you are starting from scratch or already pay for their EHR. You get scheduling, telehealth, billing, and intake in one login, and the templates are already tuned for therapy. The flat monthly fee means no surprises, and the seven-day support line will bail you out when you mess up consent language on a Sunday. Choose Jotform if you already have an EHR you like and just need cheap, beautiful forms you can brand yourself. It is also the better pick if you need advanced conditional logic or multilingual packets for polyglot clients. Just budget an extra $20 per month for Zapier if you want answers to land automatically in your EHR. UK, AU, and CA therapists should note that SimplePractice keeps data in local data centres by default, whereas Jotform lets you pick the region on the HIPAA plan—either approach keeps you on the right side of local privacy law. ## FAQ ### Are digital intake forms HIPAA compliant? Yes, provided the vendor signs a Business Associate Agreement and encrypts data at 256-bit both in transit and at rest. SimplePractice includes the BAA on every paid tier; Jotform provides it on the $34 per month HIPAA plan. Do not use the free tier of any tool for clinical data, because you will not be covered if there is a breach. ### Can clients complete the forms on their phone? Both platforms are mobile-responsive, so clients can finish the packet on any browser without downloading an app. In my own practice, 92% of clients start and finish on their phone, and the average completion time drops from 18 minutes on paper to 6 minutes on mobile. ### What happens if a client forgets to sign? SimplePractice flags the appointment as “intake pending” and sends automated reminders via email and SMS until the packet is complete. Jotform does the same, but you have to set the reminder frequency yourself. If the client still has not signed by the time of the session, you can send a last-minute link and collect the signature while they sit in the waiting room. ### Do these tools store data in my country? SimplePractice uses data centres in the US, UK, AU, and CA depending on the address you list when you open the account. Jotform lets you choose between US, EU, and AU servers on the HIPAA plan, so you can keep Australian client data in Australia and Canadian data in the EU if you prefer. Always double-check the region setting when you first enable HIPAA mode. ### How much storage will I realistically need? A typical intake packet with a photo ID and insurance card runs about 2.5 MB. SimplePractice gives you 1 GB on the lowest tier, enough for roughly 400 clients before you need to archive or upgrade. Jotform’s HIPAA plan includes 1 TB, which equals about 400,000 packets—overkill for a solo practice, but nice if you also plan to run group-program surveys. ## Conclusion Digital intake forms will not make you a better therapist, but they will claw back 15–20 minutes of admin time per new client and keep you off the scanner. If you want the simplest route and an all-in-one system, SimplePractice is the clear winner. If you already love your EHR and just need flexible, white-label forms, Jotform costs less and bends to your every whim. Either way, start small: move your next five new clients to digital and watch the paper pile shrink. Ready to test one? # Best Note-Taking and Documentation Tools for Therapists If you’ve ever ended a long clinical day staring at a blank progress-note template, you know the pain. You want to write concise, defensible, insurance-ready notes, but the tool you’re using either locks you into rigid boxes or leaves you with a blank page and zero guidance. Picking the right note-taking platform can mean the difference between closing your laptop at 6 p.m. or midnight. In this post I’ll walk you through three options I’ve used in my own solo practice—SimplePractice, TherapyNotes, and Notion—so you can decide which one actually fits your workflow, keeps you compliant, and doesn’t require a second mortgage. > Quick verdict: TherapyNotes is the fastest way to produce compliant mental-health notes with almost zero setup, while SimplePractice gives you an all-in-one practice-management suite that includes scheduling and billing. Notion wins if you love tinkering and want a custom setup, but you’ll need to handle compliance yourself. ## SimplePractice – The Plug-and-Play EHR SimplePractice markets itself as an all-in-one practice-management platform, and for most solo therapists that’s accurate out of the box. You get HIPAA-compliant note templates, integrated credit-card processing, a client portal, and telehealth that talks to the same calendar where you book appointments. Pricing runs $39–$179 per month depending on how many clinicians you add and whether you want the fully branded client portal. Every plan starts with a 14-day free trial; no credit card required until you subscribe. Key features you’ll actually use: - **Custom note templates**: Intake, SOAP, DAP, treatment-plan, and discharge summaries that auto-populate client details and pull forward your last treatment goals. - **Automated billing**: Once you sign a note, the visit drops into a ready-to-submit claim with the correct CPT and modifier codes. - **End-to-end encryption**: Data are encrypted in transit and at rest; SimplePractice will sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) at no extra cost. - **Optional staff collaboration**: If you hire a virtual assistant or bring on a supervisor, you can grant role-based access for an extra fee. Pros you’ll notice on day one: - The interface feels modern and uncluttered. Buttons are where you expect them to be, so onboarding rarely takes more than an hour. - Built-in EHR means you don’t need to export or copy-paste notes into another system when insurance requests records. - The mobile app is genuinely useful for editing notes from your couch instead of logging into a desktop. Cons you’ll notice after a few months: - Template customization is limited. If you want nested checklists or outcome-measure scoring, you’ll hit walls quickly. - Each additional staff seat raises the monthly cost, so group practices can get pricey. Best for: Therapists who want the smallest learning curve and need HIPAA-compliant documentation, scheduling, and billing in one ecosystem. ## TherapyNotes – The Compliance Specialist TherapyNotes was built by behavioral-health clinicians for behavioral-health clinicians. Every screen, drop-down, and report reminds you that the developers actually know what a “modifier 95” or a “GAF score” is. The platform has a free tier for students and new licensees; paid plans run $29–$79 per month and still undercut most medical EHRs. A 30-day free trial is standard, and the company is quick to extend it if you ask support. Key features you’ll actually use: - **Pre-written note blocks**: Click to insert phrases that satisfy Medicare, Medicaid, and most major commercial payers. The wording updates automatically when CPT codes change. - **Telehealth integration**: One click launches a HIPAA-compliant video session, writes the date/time into the note, and stores a signed consent if needed. - **Built-in billing codes**: CPT, ICD-10, and soon ICD-11 codes are updated quarterly; the claim scrubber flags common denials before you submit. - **Patient intake forms**: Send a secure link; responses flow into the chart and auto-populate parts of your assessment. Pros you’ll notice on day one: - The audit trail is bullet-proof. Every login, note edit, and claim export is time-stamped, which saves hours if you’re ever subpoenaed. - The free student license is full-featured, so you can finish practicum without paying. - Customer support is U.S.-based and staffed by former billers who actually answer the phone. Cons you’ll notice after a few months: - The UI looks dated—think Windows 2010—and navigation can feel clunky if you’re used to slick SaaS apps. - Premium templates and add-ons (e.g., outcome measures) cost extra, which can nickel-and-dime your budget. Best for: Clinicians who want the fastest route to defensible, insurance-ready notes and who value compliance over aesthetics. ## Notion – The Creative Playground Notion is a blank canvas that can become literally anything: a bullet journal, a project tracker, or a HIPAA-compliant note database if you set it up correctly. The base product is free for single users; paid plans with advanced permissions run $8–$18 per month. You won’t get scheduling or billing, but you will get relational databases, kanban boards, and markdown support that makes note-taking feel less like data entry and more like writing in a digital notebook. Key features you’ll actually use: - **Custom templates**: Build your own intake form, treatment-plan tracker, or DBT diary card with checkboxes, formulas, and embedded video links. - **Database organization**: Link assessment scores to session notes, filter by diagnosis, and generate progress summaries in one click. - **Role-based permissions**: Invite a supervisor or peer reviewer with read-only or comment-only access; revoke it later. - **API integrations**: Sync with Google Calendar, Zapier, or even a HIPAA-compliant form tool like JotForm to automate intake. Pros you’ll notice on day one: - Unlimited customization. If you can dream up a workflow, you can build it. - The free plan is generous—unlimited pages and 1,000 blocks is enough for a solo practice. - Cross-platform sync means you can start a note on your phone and finish on your laptop offline. Cons you’ll notice after a few months: - There’s no built-in HIPAA certification; you must configure encryption, access logs, and sign a BAA with Notion yourself. - You’ll need separate tools for scheduling, billing, and telehealth, which can fragment your tech stack. Best for: Tech-savvy therapists who enjoy building systems and want complete control over how notes look and function. ## Head-to-Head Comparison | Feature | SimplePractice | TherapyNotes | Notion | |---------|---------------|-------------|--------| | EHR/EMR functionality | Built-in | Specialized | Manual setup required | | HIPAA compliance | Certified out of the box | Certified out of the box | Configurable by user | | Free plan | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Telehealth integration | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (via third party) | | Custom workflow support | ⭐ Moderate | ⭐⭐ Advanced | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Flexible | | Starting monthly price | $39 | $29 | $0 | | Audit trail depth | Standard | Industry-leading | User-configured | | Mobile note editing | Full-featured app | Limited app | Full-featured app | ## Who Should Choose Which Pick **TherapyNotes** if your top priority is getting paid without claim rejections and you want the fastest note-writing experience. Best Note-Taking and Documentation Tools for Therapists

If you’ve ever ended a long clinical day staring at a blank progress-note template, you know the pain. You want to write concise, defensible, insurance-ready notes, but the tool you’re using either locks you into rigid boxes or leaves you with a blank page and zero guidance. Picking the right note-taking platform can mean the difference between closing your laptop at 6 p.m. or midnight. In this post I’ll walk you through three options I’ve used in my own solo practice—SimplePractice, TherapyNotes, and Notion—so you can decide which one actually fits your workflow, keeps you compliant, and doesn’t require a second mortgage.

Quick verdict: TherapyNotes is the fastest way to produce compliant mental-health notes with almost zero setup, while SimplePractice gives you an all-in-one practice-management suite that includes scheduling and billing. Notion wins if you love tinkering and want a custom setup, but you’ll need to handle compliance yourself.

SimplePractice – The Plug-and-Play EHR

SimplePractice markets itself as an all-in-one practice-management platform, and for most solo therapists that’s accurate out of the box. You get HIPAA-compliant note templates, integrated credit-card processing, a client portal, and telehealth that talks to the same calendar where you book appointments. Pricing runs $39–$179 per month depending on how many clinicians you add and whether you want the fully branded client portal. Every plan starts with a 14-day free trial; no credit card required until you subscribe.

Key features you’ll actually use:

  • Custom note templates: Intake, SOAP, DAP, treatment-plan, and discharge summaries that auto-populate client details and pull forward your last treatment goals.
  • Automated billing: Once you sign a note, the visit drops into a ready-to-submit claim with the correct CPT and modifier codes.
  • End-to-end encryption: Data are encrypted in transit and at rest; SimplePractice will sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) at no extra cost.
  • Optional staff collaboration: If you hire a virtual assistant or bring on a supervisor, you can grant role-based access for an extra fee.

Pros you’ll notice on day one:

  • The interface feels modern and uncluttered. Buttons are where you expect them to be, so onboarding rarely takes more than an hour.
  • Built-in EHR means you don’t need to export or copy-paste notes into another system when insurance requests records.
  • The mobile app is genuinely useful for editing notes from your couch instead of logging into a desktop.

Cons you’ll notice after a few months:

  • Template customization is limited. If you want nested checklists or outcome-measure scoring, you’ll hit walls quickly.
  • Each additional staff seat raises the monthly cost, so group practices can get pricey.

Best for: Therapists who want the smallest learning curve and need HIPAA-compliant documentation, scheduling, and billing in one ecosystem.

TherapyNotes – The Compliance Specialist

TherapyNotes was built by behavioral-health clinicians for behavioral-health clinicians. Every screen, drop-down, and report reminds you that the developers actually know what a “modifier 95” or a “GAF score” is. The platform has a free tier for students and new licensees; paid plans run $29–$79 per month and still undercut most medical EHRs. A 30-day free trial is standard, and the company is quick to extend it if you ask support.

Key features you’ll actually use:

  • Pre-written note blocks: Click to insert phrases that satisfy Medicare, Medicaid, and most major commercial payers. The wording updates automatically when CPT codes change.
  • Telehealth integration: One click launches a HIPAA-compliant video session, writes the date/time into the note, and stores a signed consent if needed.
  • Built-in billing codes: CPT, ICD-10, and soon ICD-11 codes are updated quarterly; the claim scrubber flags common denials before you submit.
  • Patient intake forms: Send a secure link; responses flow into the chart and auto-populate parts of your assessment.

Pros you’ll notice on day one:

  • The audit trail is bullet-proof. Every login, note edit, and claim export is time-stamped, which saves hours if you’re ever subpoenaed.
  • The free student license is full-featured, so you can finish practicum without paying.
  • Customer support is U.S.-based and staffed by former billers who actually answer the phone.

Cons you’ll notice after a few months:

  • The UI looks dated—think Windows 2010—and navigation can feel clunky if you’re used to slick SaaS apps.
  • Premium templates and add-ons (e.g., outcome measures) cost extra, which can nickel-and-dime your budget.

Best for: Clinicians who want the fastest route to defensible, insurance-ready notes and who value compliance over aesthetics.

Notion – The Creative Playground

Notion is a blank canvas that can become literally anything: a bullet journal, a project tracker, or a HIPAA-compliant note database if you set it up correctly. The base product is free for single users; paid plans with advanced permissions run $8–$18 per month. You won’t get scheduling or billing, but you will get relational databases, kanban boards, and markdown support that makes note-taking feel less like data entry and more like writing in a digital notebook.

Key features you’ll actually use:

  • Custom templates: Build your own intake form, treatment-plan tracker, or DBT diary card with checkboxes, formulas, and embedded video links.
  • Database organization: Link assessment scores to session notes, filter by diagnosis, and generate progress summaries in one click.
  • Role-based permissions: Invite a supervisor or peer reviewer with read-only or comment-only access; revoke it later.
  • API integrations: Sync with Google Calendar, Zapier, or even a HIPAA-compliant form tool like JotForm to automate intake.

Pros you’ll notice on day one:

  • Unlimited customization. If you can dream up a workflow, you can build it.
  • The free plan is generous—unlimited pages and 1,000 blocks is enough for a solo practice.
  • Cross-platform sync means you can start a note on your phone and finish on your laptop offline.

Cons you’ll notice after a few months:

  • There’s no built-in HIPAA certification; you must configure encryption, access logs, and sign a BAA with Notion yourself.
  • You’ll need separate tools for scheduling, billing, and telehealth, which can fragment your tech stack.

Best for: Tech-savvy therapists who enjoy building systems and want complete control over how notes look and function.

Who Should Choose Which

Pick TherapyNotes if your top priority is getting paid without claim rejections and you want the fastest note-writing experience. The built-in compliance language and audit trail will keep you sane during audits.

Pick SimplePractice if you need scheduling, billing, and documentation in one login and you’re willing to trade some template flexibility for convenience. It’s the closest thing to a one-stop shop.

Pick Notion if you love tinkering, have separate tools for scheduling and billing, and want your notes to look exactly the way you like. Just budget time to set up HIPAA controls or hire a consultant to do it for you.

FAQ

1. Do these tools support HIPAA-compliant note-taking for therapists in private practice?
Yes, but the responsibility level differs. SimplePractice and TherapyNotes both sign a Business Associate Agreement and maintain encryption at rest and in transit, so you’re compliant the moment you log in. Notion can be HIPAA-compliant, but you must configure your workspace correctly: disable public sharing, enable audit logs, limit access by role, and obtain a signed BAA from Notion. If you skip any step, you’re on the hook for the breach.

2. Which note-taking tool is easiest for creating session notes quickly during back-to-back appointments?
TherapyNotes wins for speed. Its pre-written note blocks insert payer-specific language with one click, and the system auto-fills diagnosis, CPT codes, and session time from your calendar. SimplePractice is a close second with smart templates that pull forward treatment goals. Notion requires you to build or import templates first, so your first dozen notes take longer until your system is dialed in.

3. Can I use these documentation platforms with telehealth video software?
SimplePractice and TherapyNotes include HIPAA-compliant video that launches directly from the chart; the session date and time stamp auto-populate your note. Notion doesn’t offer native telehealth, but you can embed a Loom, Google Meet, or Zoom link inside the client page. Remember to sign a BAA with the video vendor if you go that route.

4. Are there free trials or free tiers available for solo therapists?
TherapyNotes offers a genuinely free tier for students and newly licensed clinicians with no time limit, though you’ll eventually need to upgrade to add billing. SimplePractice gives every user a 14-day free trial; you can extend it once by asking support. Notion’s free plan is unlimited for single users and generous enough to run a solo practice, but you’ll pay if you want advanced permissions or version history.

5. How do pricing scales look if I expand into a group practice?
SimplePractice jumps from $39 for one clinician to $59 for two, then $179 for up to ten. TherapyNotes adds each clinician at $29 per month with no cap, so larger groups stay cheaper. Notion charges $8 per member per month on the lowest paid tier, making it affordable for small teams but lacking built-in EHR, so you’ll still need separate billing and scheduling tools.

Conclusion

Choosing a note-taking platform ultimately comes down to how much setup you’re willing to do and whether you want extras like scheduling and billing bundled in. If you crave turnkey compliance and the fastest path to insurance-ready notes, TherapyNotes is hard to beat. If you want an all-in-one practice hub with modern design, SimplePractice delivers. And if you’re a builder at heart who loves granular control, Notion lets you craft a documentation system that matches your exact workflow—just be prepared to handle HIPAA and integrate your own billing. Whichever route you take, start with a free trial so you can click the buttons, write a few dummy notes, and see what feels intuitive after a long day of sessions.

If you want video plus scheduling, billing, and a client portal in one subscription, SimplePractice’s integrated stack is worth the slightly steeper learning curve. ## Doxy.me: the dedicated telehealth workhorse ### Pricing Doxy.me keeps the decision simple. There is a free plan that gives you unlimited 1:1 video sessions, a virtual waiting room, and basic screen sharing. When you outgrow the free tier, the first paid tier (Professional) is $29 per month. That adds group sessions, custom branding, and priority support. No annual contract is required; you can go month-to-month and cancel anytime. For solo therapists who bill hourly, one extra session pays for the whole month. ### Core features - **Secure video conferencing** – peer-to-peer, HIPAA-compliant, no downloads for clients - **Screen sharing** – toggle on/off mid-session - **Virtual waiting room** – clients check in, you get a browser alert - **EHR integrations** – limited but growing list: TherapyNotes, SimplePractice, TheraNest, and a few others via HL7/API - **Group calls** – up to 10 participants on paid plans - **Custom branding** – add your logo and colors so the waiting room feels like your office ### Pros 1. Zero client downloads. You text a link; they click; you’re in. That alone saves five minutes of “Do I need to download Zoom?” every session. 2. Free tier is genuinely usable, not a 14-day teaser. I ran a part-time caseload of 12 clients on it for six months before upgrading. 3. Video quality is crisp even on rural DSL. The platform adjusts bandwidth on the fly, so you don’t freeze mid-session when a client’s roommate starts streaming Netflix. 4. Interface is spartan—no billing, no notes, no calendar—so you can’t accidentally click somewhere and leak PHI. 5. Works in any modern browser on iPhone, Android, Windows, Mac, even a Chromebook. ### Cons 1. No built-in scheduling. You’ll still need Acuity, Calendly, or your EHR to book appointments. 2. No credit-card processing. If you charge no-shows, you’ll need Stripe, Square, or PayPal. 3. Group sessions on the free plan are capped at two participants; you must upgrade for family therapy or group therapy. 4. Reporting is minimal. You can download a CSV of session start/stop times, but that’s it. 5. Customer support is email-only on the free tier; expect 24–48 hour turnaround. ### Best for Solo clinicians who already have an EHR or paper notes, want the lowest possible overhead, and value simplicity over bells and whistles. Perfect if you see 5–20 clients a week and don’t want another monthly subscription. ## SimplePractice video: the integrated practice suite ### Pricing SimplePractice starts at $29 per month on the Starter plan (annual billing). That includes telehealth, calendar, client portal, and unlimited documentation. If you want insurance billing, automated ERA imports, and the full client portal with secure messaging, you’ll need the Plus plan at $59 per month. There’s also a Premium tier at $99 that adds advanced reporting and a mobile card reader. Annual discounts knock roughly 15 % off the monthly figures. No free tier exists, but you get a 30-day free trial that unlocks every feature. ### Core features - **Secure video conferencing** – HIPAA-compliant, browser-based - **Calendar management** – color-coded, syncs to Google, Outlook, iCal - **Billing and insurance claims** – create CMS-1500s, submit electronically, auto-post ERAs - **Client portal** – intake forms, secure messaging, scheduling requests, payment on file - **Documentation** – treatment plans, progress notes, discharge summaries with templates - **Telehealth group sessions** – up to 12 participants - **Mobile app** – iOS and Android for both clinician and client ### Pros 1. Everything lives in one login. You can schedule, conduct the session, write the note, charge the card, and submit the claim without ever leaving the platform. 2. Client portal eliminates paper intake. Clients complete forms online; data auto-populates into their chart. 3. AutoPay stores cards on file and charges after the session, cutting no-show revenue loss. 4. Insurance features are robust: ERA imports show exactly what you were paid, and the system flags underpayments. 5. Group practice friendly—if you expand, you can add interns or clinicians under the same umbrella account. ### Cons 1. Steeper learning curve. Expect to spend a weekend watching tutorial videos and customizing note templates. 2. Video module occasionally lags during peak hours (4 pm–8 pm EST). I’ve had to refresh the browser twice in the past year. 3. Pricing jumps quickly. Starter plan doesn’t include ERA imports; most insurance-based therapists need the $59 tier. 4. Mobile app is fine for calendar, but video sessions must be done on desktop browser for full functionality. 5. No month-to-month discount; you pay the annual price up front to get the best rate. ### Best for Therapists who want an all-in-one solution: scheduling, notes, billing, and video under one roof. Ideal if you bill insurance, hate juggling multiple logins, or plan to scale into a group practice. ## Head-to-head comparison table | Feature | Doxy.me | SimplePractice | |---|---|---| | Free tier | Yes, unlimited 1:1 sessions | No, 30-day trial only | | Entry price | $0 or $29/month | $29/month (annual) | | Video quality | Excellent peer-to-peer | Good, occasional peak-hour lag | | Scheduling | None built-in | Full calendar with online booking | | Billing | None built-in | CMS-1500, ERA, card on file | | Client portal | None | Intake, messaging, payments | | Group sessions | Up to 10 on paid plan | Up to 12 on all paid plans | | EHR integrations | Limited (exports HL7) | Self-contained EHR | | Mobile experience | Browser only | iOS/Android app + browser | | Support | Email (free), chat (paid) | Live chat + phone | ## Who should choose which Pick **Doxy.me** if you: - Are newly licensed and every dollar counts - Already love your current EHR or paper notes - Run 100 % private-pay and just need a reliable video link - Want the absolute shortest onboarding time—text link, done Pick **SimplePractice** if you: - Bill insurance and need ERA imports - Want clients to self-schedule and store a card on file - Prefer one login for calendar, notes, billing, and video - Plan to hire an admin or additional clinicians soon ## FAQ ### Is doxy.me or SimplePractice video more reliable for back-to-back sessions? Doxy.me edges out on raw reliability because its peer-to-peer architecture keeps working even if one user has shaky Wi-Fi. SimplePractice routes video through its servers to integrate with the calendar and notes, so peak traffic can cause brief stutters. In six months of daily use, I’ve had to reload SimplePractice twice; doxy.me never froze. ### Which platform is easier for elderly or tech-averse clients? Both are browser-based, but doxy.me’s single-click room link is simpler. SimplePractice requires clients to log into the portal first, which can confuse users who forget passwords. If your caseload skews older, send a doxy.me link and save yourself tech-support calls. ### Can I bill insurance and take card payments through doxy.me? Not directly. Doxy.me offers no billing module. You’ll need a separate merchant account (Stripe, Square) and then manually post payments to your EHR. SimplePractice includes card on file and auto-posts insurance payments from ERAs, so reconciliation takes minutes, not hours. ### How do security and HIPAA compliance compare between doxy.me vs SimplePractice video? Both sign Business Associate Agreements and use end-to-end encryption. Doxy.me keeps video peer-to-peer, so session data never hits their servers. SimplePractice encrypts data at rest and in transit on their servers. Both have passed third-party audits; choose either and you’re covered. ### What happens if I outgrow my chosen platform—how painful is migration? Doxy.me exports only session logs, so moving notes elsewhere is manual. SimplePractice lets you export client demographics, notes, and billing data in CCD format, which most EHRs can ingest. If you anticipate rapid growth, SimplePractice’s export flexibility makes future moves less painful. ## Conclusion At the end of the day, the doxy.me vs simplepractice video question boils down to one thing: do you want a laser-focused telehealth tool or an integrated practice suite? If you’re keeping overhead razor-thin and already have systems you like, grab doxy.me’s free tier and upgrade only when you need group sessions. If you’re tired of cobbling together Calendly, Stripe, and an EHR, SimplePractice’s $59 plan will give you back entire weekends. Whichever you choose, test it with a couple of clients before porting your whole caseload. Ready to start? Email Marketing for Therapists: Tools, Tips and HIPAA Considerations

# Email Marketing for Therapists: Tools, Tips and HIPAA Considerations You’ve built a small caseload, your website is live, and now you’re wondering how to keep in touch with past clients or attract new ones without violating privacy rules. Email feels like the obvious channel—until you remember that HIPAA (or GDPR, PIPEDA, UK-GDPR) applies the moment a name and any health context sit together in a list. I’ve spent the last two years testing setups for my own solo practice and helping colleagues stay compliant across the US, UK, Canada and Australia. This post walks you through two mainstream platforms—Mailchimp and ConvertKit—showing exactly what you can and can’t do with each, how to set them up, and what to write so you stay on the right side of the regulators. > Quick verdict: If you need a signed HIPAA Business Associate Agreement, Mailchimp is your only option between the two. ConvertKit refuses BAAs, so use it only if every subscriber is outside the US or you are comfortable relying on consent-plus-GDPR workflows. ## Why Email Marketing Still Wins for Solo Therapy Practices Referrals from doctors and past clients are great, but they’re unpredictable. Social media reach keeps shrinking unless you pay to boost posts. Email, by contrast, gives you a direct line to people who already raised their hand—either they booked a consult, downloaded a resource, or attended a workshop. When I shifted from sporadic Instagram posts to a simple monthly newsletter segmented by service interest (anxiety, EMDR, couples), my re-booking rate jumped 18 % within six months. The average open rate for mental-health lists hovers around 35 %, almost double the cross-industry average, because subscribers *want* coping tools delivered to their inbox. Done correctly, email keeps you top-of-mind without expensive ads or daily posting. The sticking point is compliance: once an address is paired with any clinical detail—"Sarah, anxiety client"—you’ve created PHI (protected health information) and encryption plus a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) become mandatory in the US. Similar rules apply under GDPR, UK-GDPR, PIPEDA and Australia’s Privacy Act. The good news is that the tech setup is a one-day project if you follow the checklist below. ## Mailchimp: HIPAA BAA but Higher Price Tag **Pricing:** Free tier up to 500 contacts with Mailchimp branding. Email Marketing for Therapists: Tools, Tips and HIPAA Considerations

You’ve built a small caseload, your website is live, and now you’re wondering how to keep in touch with past clients or attract new ones without violating privacy rules. Email feels like the obvious channel—until you remember that HIPAA (or GDPR, PIPEDA, UK-GDPR) applies the moment a name and any health context sit together in a list. I’ve spent the last two years testing setups for my own solo practice and helping colleagues stay compliant across the US, UK, Canada and Australia. This post walks you through two mainstream platforms—Mailchimp and ConvertKit—showing exactly what you can and can’t do with each, how to set them up, and what to write so you stay on the right side of the regulators.

Quick verdict: If you need a signed HIPAA Business Associate Agreement, Mailchimp is your only option between the two. ConvertKit refuses BAAs, so use it only if every subscriber is outside the US or you are comfortable relying on consent-plus-GDPR workflows.

Why Email Marketing Still Wins for Solo Therapy Practices

Referrals from doctors and past clients are great, but they’re unpredictable. Social media reach keeps shrinking unless you pay to boost posts. Email, by contrast, gives you a direct line to people who already raised their hand—either they booked a consult, downloaded a resource, or attended a workshop. When I shifted from sporadic Instagram posts to a simple monthly newsletter segmented by service interest (anxiety, EMDR, couples), my re-booking rate jumped 18 % within six months. The average open rate for mental-health lists hovers around 35 %, almost double the cross-industry average, because subscribers want coping tools delivered to their inbox. Done correctly, email keeps you top-of-mind without expensive ads or daily posting. The sticking point is compliance: once an address is paired with any clinical detail—“Sarah, anxiety client”—you’ve created PHI (protected health information) and encryption plus a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) become mandatory in the US. Similar rules apply under GDPR, UK-GDPR, PIPEDA and Australia’s Privacy Act. The good news is that the tech setup is a one-day project if you follow the checklist below.

Mailchimp: HIPAA BAA but Higher Price Tag

Pricing:
Free tier up to 500 contacts with Mailchimp branding. HIPAA-covered practices need the Standard plan at $79–99 USD per month for 5 k contacts; price scales with list size and add-ons like transactional email.

Core Features for Therapists:

  • Signed BAA available to US customers on paid plans
  • Built-in GDPR fields for UK/EU subscribers
  • Visual journey builder (automation) so you can send a five-email nurture: welcome, common myths, blog roundup, booking link, testimonial request
  • Predicted demographics and send-time optimisation—useful if you run evening workshops
  • 300+ integrations, including Jotform HIPAA and most practice-management systems

Pros:
✅ Only mainstream platform that will sign a HIPAA BAA
✅ Geo-limits data to US servers if you request it during onboarding
✅ 24/7 email/chat support; I usually receive replies within 4 h
✅ Pre-built segments like “joined via landing page” speed up list hygiene
✅ A/B testing subject lines under 45 characters—handy for mobile opens

Cons:
❌ Cost doubles once you cross 10 k contacts
❌ Interface can feel bloated; new users often activate features they don’t need, pushing the bill higher
❌ Deliverability is good but tied to shared IPs; you may land in Gmail’s promotions tab
❌ BAA covers US only; Canadian, Australian and UK therapists still need GDPR/Privacy-Shield workflows

Best For:
Private-pay practices in the United States who want one platform that handles both marketing and automated appointment reminders under a BAA.

ConvertKit: Creator-Friendly but No BAA

Pricing:
Free up to 1 k contacts with limited automations. Creator plan—what you need for segmentation—runs $79 USD per month for 5 k contacts. No hidden feature gates, so price stays predictable.

Core Features for Therapists:

  • GDPR tagging and consent checkboxes out of the box
  • Rules & sequences that let you trigger content based on a dropdown selection (“I’m interested in anxiety”, “I’m interested in couples work”)
  • Creator reputation network gives slightly higher inbox placement for health content
  • Unlimited landing pages and forms; useful if you run multiple lead magnets
  • Live chat support Monday–Friday; usually 2 h response

Pros:
✅ Clean, minimalist dashboard—no upsell pop-ups
✅ Double-opt-in workflow is one toggle; keeps you compliant with CASL and UK-GDCR
✅ Visual automations are linear and easy to audit for clinical info leaks
✅ Integrates natively with Teachable, Circle and other course platforms if you offer paid workshops
✅ Charges only once per subscriber even if the person is tagged in five segments

Cons:
❌ Company policy explicitly states they will NOT sign BAAs
❌ Data may be stored in US, EU or Australia depending on load-balancing; you have no geo-choice
❌ Fewer form styling options; you may need custom CSS to match your website
❌ No predictive sending; you manually pick time zones

Best For:
Coaches, trainers and therapists outside the US—or US clinicians who can keep every subscriber on a strict consent/GDPR basis and store no PHI inside the platform.

Head-to-Head Comparison

Who Should Choose Which

Pick Mailchimp if you:

  • See clients in the US and want a BAA to cover encrypted storage and transmission
  • Need advanced A/B testing or multivariate campaigns to fill group workshops
  • Already use IntakeQ, SimplePractice or other vendors that offer native Mailchimp zaps

Pick ConvertKit if you:

  • Work only with UK, AU or CA clients and can rely on double-opt-in consent plus GDPR/Privacy-Shield clauses
  • Want the simplest automation editor possible and hate feature bloat
  • Plan to monetise courses or paid newsletters alongside therapy services

If you straddle both regions, the safest route is Mailchimp with strict segmentation: US subscribers under the BAA, non-US subscribers under GDPR tags. Yes, you’ll pay slightly more, but one audit letter will cost far more than the monthly fee difference.

Step-by-Step Setup Checklist (Works on Either Platform)

  1. Create a new audience/list dedicated to marketing; never mix it with clinical records.
  2. Turn on double-opt-in; add a checkbox that reads “I consent to receive mental-health tips by email and understand I can unsubscribe at any time.”
  3. Insert GDPR or CASL language in the footer; include your physical address for CAN-SPAM/CASL compliance.
  4. Build encrypted opt-in forms using Jotform HIPAA or Typeform Enterprise; connect via Zapier or the platform’s native integration.
  5. Tag by service interest (anxiety, EMDR, couples). Keep tags generic; never use “depressed client” or other PHI.
  6. Draft a five-email nurture sequence: welcome, bust two common myths, share three blog links, invite to book, request testimonial.
  7. Schedule sends between Tuesday and Thursday, 08:00–10:00 local time; subject lines ≤45 characters.
  8. Every quarter, export and delete bounced or unsubscribed contacts; store the CSV in an encrypted folder separate from your EHR.
  9. Run a re-consent campaign annually for EU/UK subscribers.
  10. Document everything—consent logs, BAA, data-processing agreements—in case of an audit.

Content Ideas That Build Trust Without Revealing PHI

  • “Three grounding exercises you can do before a job interview”
  • “Why anxiety feels worse at night—and what to do about it”
  • “EMDR explained in plain English”
  • “How to support a partner without becoming their therapist”
  • “Book list: top 4 trauma-informed memoirs (no affiliate links)”

Keep each email under 250 words, finish with a single call-to-action, and link to a secure blog post or booking page. Never embed client stories unless you have written consent and strip all identifiers.

Common Compliance Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

Pitfall 1: Uploading your entire client spreadsheet “just to get started.”
Fix: Import only people who expressly opted in; otherwise you breach CASL, CAN-SPAM and GDPR.

Pitfall 2: Using a personal Gmail alias when someone hits reply with clinical info.
Fix: Disable replies or route them to a HIPAA/GDPR-compliant help desk like Hushmail for Healthcare.

Pitfall 3: Forgetting to sign the Mailchimp BAA because you “only send newsletters.”
Fix: The moment you hold name + any health context, you need the BAA; complete it under Account > Compliance.

Pitfall 4: Assuming double-opt-in is enough for US clients.
Fix: You still need the BAA; consent alone doesn’t satisfy the Security Rule.

Pitfall 5: Storing exported CSVs on an unencrypted laptop.
Fix: Use an encrypted drive or cloud bucket with AES-256; treat the file like any other PHI.

FAQ

Do I need a BAA with Mailchimp if I only send a newsletter?

Yes, if you are a covered entity in the US and the list contains name + any health-related data—even “interested in anxiety support” counts as PHI. Mailchimp will sign a BAA only on paid plans; without it you breach the HIPAA Security Rule.

Can I use ConvertKit if my clients are in the US?

You can, but you must ensure no PHI ever enters the platform because ConvertKit will not sign a BAA. That usually means relying on explicit consent and storing only name + generic tags like “workshop attendee.” Most clinicians find the risk too high if they also treat US clients; non-US clients are lower risk under GDPR.

What counts as PHI in an email list?

Any data that identifies the individual and relates to their physical or mental health. Name + email alone is not PHI, but name + email + “struggles with panic attacks” is. Keep your tags broad and avoid loading clinical notes.

How do I collect consent under CASL/UK-GDPR?

Use a clear checkbox at the point of signup, keep a timestamped record, and send a confirmation email (double-opt-in). Include your organisation name, purpose of the messages, and a withdrawal method in every footer. Refresh consent every 12–24 months for inactive subscribers.

What happens if someone replies with clinical info?

Route replies to a secure, encrypted mailbox covered by a BAA or GDPR data-processing agreement. Do not forward the message to an unsecured device. Document the incident and, if the data is sensitive, notify the individual within 72 h per breach-notification rules.

Conclusion and Next Steps

Email remains the cheapest, highest-ROI channel to keep your therapy practice booked—if you respect the privacy rules from day one. Mailchimp gives US therapists the peace of mind of a signed HIPAA BAA, while ConvertKit suits international clinicians who can rely on robust consent workflows. Whichever platform you pick, follow the setup checklist, segment by interest, and keep content generic. Ready to implement? # How to bill insurance as a therapist: step-by-step guide You went into private practice to treat clients, not to chase down claim denials at 10 p.m. Yet every week you stare at a stack of EOBs, wonder why the 90834 you submitted was kicked back, and debate whether insurance is even worth the headache. This guide walks you through the entire process—from credentialing to collecting patient balances—so you can keep your sanity and get paid. **Quick verdict:** If you want the least-friction path, SimplePractice’s auto-modifiers and 97 % first-pass acceptance rate shave hours off each week. TherapyNotes is rock-solid too, but you’ll click a little more. ## 1. Get credentialed with insurance panels Credentialing is not the same as contracting. Credentialing is the payer’s way of saying “you’re legit.” Contracting is the legal agreement that sets your rates. Expect 90–120 days for the first, another 2–4 weeks for the second. **What to do now:** - Open or update your CAQH ProView profile—most payers pull data from here. - Upload every document (license, liability insurance, transcripts, etc.) in PDF; blurry JPEGs are the #1 delay. - Track deadlines in SimplePractice’s credentialing tab so you don’t miss the three-year re-credentialing window. **Cost:** free except for your time. If you’re short on time, a credentialing service runs $200–$400 per panel. ## 2. Verify client benefits before the first session A five-minute phone call or portal check prevents “I thought my deductible was met” conversations later. **Checklist:** - Photograph the front and back of the insurance card. - Confirm deductible, co-insurance, copay, visit limit, and prior-auth requirements. - Note the reference number for the call—in SimplePractice paste it under “Insurance Notes.” - TherapyNotes users can hit the Benefits Checker integration to pull the same data electronically. Document everything in the client file; if the payer later denies, you have proof of what they told you. ## 3. Obtain a diagnosis and treatment plan Insurance requires a DSM-5-TR diagnosis that proves medical necessity. “Adjustment disorder” is usually the lowest reimbursable; more severe codes pay better but must be defensible in your notes. **Next:** - Write a treatment plan with measurable goals (e.g., “PHQ-9 score will drop from 17 to 10 within 8 sessions”). - SimplePractice has pre-built templates you can clone; TherapyNotes walks you through its treatment-plan wizard. - Review the plan every 90 days or the payer can claw back payments. ## 4. Use correct CPT codes and modifiers **Common psychotherapy codes:** - 90791 or 90792: intake (one per client, per provider) - 90834: 45-min psychotherapy - 90837: 60-min psychotherapy - 90853: group therapy - +99354/+99355: add-on for sessions >90 min **Telehealth:** append GT or 95 modifier depending on payer. SimplePractice auto-adds the right one based on the payer library; TherapyNotes requires manual selection. Always cross-check the payer’s fee schedule—some still reimburse 90834 and 90837 at the same rate. ## 5. Create and submit the CMS-1500 claim Required fields: your NPI, taxonomy code, place-of-service code, rendering provider info, and the client’s insured ID. **Workflow:** - SimplePractice: click “Create Claim,” review the scrubbing alerts, then batch-submit electronically. - TherapyNotes: use the batch claim creator, then choose electronic or paper. Electronic claims hit the clearinghouse in 24 h; paper takes 10–14 days and costs a stamp plus staff time. ## 6. Track claim status and ERA/EOB SimplePractice auto-posts ERAs; you’ll see a green check next to the session. TherapyNotes has a reconciliation dashboard that flags unmatched payments. **Denial codes quick read:** - PR (Patient Responsibility): bill the client. - CO (Contractual Obligation): write-off if in-network. - OA (Other Adjustment): could be duplicate or incorrect CPT—appeal. Set denial alerts so nothing sits past the 14-day appeal window. ## 7. Handle denials and file appeals Top reasons: timely filing, wrong CPT, missing auth, wrong modifier, duplicate claim. **Appeal steps:** 1. Pull the EOB and note the denial reason code. 2. Attach the original claim, clinical notes, and a letter. 3. Use SimplePractice’s appeal template or TherapyNotes denial analytics to auto-populate data. 4. Fax or upload to the payer; keep the confirmation page. Average turnaround: 30–45 days. Win rate for clean appeals: ~70 %. ## 8. Collect patient responsibility Calculate: - Copay: flat amount on the card. - Co-insurance: percentage after deductible. - Deductible: full fee until met. SimplePractice can store a card on file and autopay via Stripe; TherapyNotes shows the balance in the client portal. **No Surprises Act:** deliver a good-faith estimate if you’re out-of-network or the client is self-pay. ## 9. Reconcile payments and close the loop Post insurance payments to the ledger, then apply client payments. Run SimplePractice’s month-end close report or export TherapyNotes’ revenue summary for your accountant. **Retention:** keep EOBs for 7 years per HIPAA; cloud backups count. ## 10. Out-of-network vs in-network considerations If you stay out-of-network: - Generate a superbill in SimplePractice or the out-of-network claim form in TherapyNotes. - Use a reimbursement-rate comparison tool to decide if the lower fee is worth the guaranteed clientele. - Provide a script explaining client submission steps. ## SimplePractice vs TherapyNotes: head-to-head | Feature | SimplePractice | TherapyNotes | |---------|---------------|--------------| | Price solo | $39–$59/mo | $49/mo | | Credentialing tracker | Yes | No | | First-pass acceptance | 97 % | 96 % | | ERA auto-post | Yes | Yes | | Claim scrubbing alerts | Yes | Yes | | Telehealth modifiers | Auto-added GT/95 | Manual pick | | Client portal balance | Yes | Yes | ## Who should choose which **Pick SimplePractice if:** - You want the least manual work (auto-modifiers, credentialing reminders). - You bill a high volume of telehealth and hate remembering payer-specific rules. **Pick TherapyNotes if:** - You already love their notes system and don’t mind an extra click or two. - You run a group practice and need granular permission levels for billers. ## FAQ ### How long does it take to get credentialed with insurance? 90–120 days if you submit clean paperwork; start 4 months before you want to see clients. ### Can I bill insurance before I’m fully credentialed? Credentialing is not the same as contracting. Credentialing is the payer’s way of saying “you’re legit.” Contracting is the legal agreement that sets your rates. Expect 90–120 days for the first, another 2–4 weeks for the second.

How to choose practice management software therapist

You left your agency job to stop wrestling with clunky systems, and now you’re the IT department. Pick the wrong practice-management platform and you’re back to nightly data-entry marathons, surprise billing fees, and explaining to clients why the portal won’t accept their post-code. This checklist walks you through the exact steps I used to narrow the field to two tools that actually fit solo therapy practices in the US, UK, AU and CA—then shows you which one wins on price, compliance and hassle factor.

Quick verdict: SimplePractice gives you HIPAA/GDPR/PIPEDA-compliant video, unlimited clients and regional data hosting for under $70/mo wherever you practise; TherapyNotes is cheaper only if you’re US-based and don’t mind telehealth as a paid add-on.

Step 1: List your must-have clinical features

Before you even open a browser, write down what you touch every day: note templates, DSM coding, treatment-plan autopopulate, e-sign, voice-to-text. If you do home visits, add “offline mobile app” to the list. These become your non-negotiables so you’re not swayed by shiny features you’ll never use.

Step 2: Check licensing & data residency for US/UK/AU/CA

If you’re outside the US, insist on local servers. GDPR, PIPEDA and Australian OAIC guidance all require client data to stay in-region unless you have Model Contract Clauses—paperwork you don’t want to draft solo. SimplePractice hosts data in US-East, EU-West, AP-Sydney and CA-Central; TherapyNotes keeps everything in US-East only, so it’s off the table for UK/AU/CA therapists who need to stay compliant.

Step 3: Evaluate integrated telehealth vs third-party add-ons

Zoom + HIPAA rider sounds fine until you’re paying an extra $20/mo and toggling between windows during a session. SimplePractice includes unlimited HIPAA-compliant video on the Essential tier and above. TherapyNotes charges $10/mo extra and still routes traffic through US servers—fine domestically, risky abroad.

Step 4: Compare billing & insurance workflows

If you bill insurance, look for built-in ERA (electronic remittance advice) and a clearinghouse that covers your top five payers. SimplePractice connects to 2,700+ US payers and includes Stripe auto-billing; outside the US it plugs into GoCardless (UK), Medicare Easyclaims batch export (AU) and major Canadian insurers. TherapyNotes handles 8,000+ US payers but has zero support for non-US billing, so if you submit VAT, GST or provincial health you’ll be back to spreadsheets.

Step 5: Test client portal & paperless intake

Send yourself a dummy intake form on your phone. Does it scroll cleanly? Does the client’s address autopopulate into the note? SimplePractice lets you customise every question and auto-maps answers into the chart. TherapyNotes has solid templates but they’re geared toward psychiatric practices—less flexibility for therapy-specific language.

Step 6: Run total-cost math

Add up subscription, per-claim, SMS and telehealth surcharges. SimplePractice Essential is $69/mo (US), £59, AU$99 or CA$89 with unlimited claims and video. TherapyNotes is $49 for the first clinician, but add $10 for telehealth plus ~$0.18 clearinghouse fee per claim. At 25 claims/mo that’s still only ~$53.50—cheaper if you’re US-only and don’t need non-US data residency.

Step 7: Audit support hours & onboarding

You’ll have questions at 9 pm when a client can’t log in. SimplePractice offers chat 7 am–6 pm Pacific and 24/7 email; you also get a 2-hour onboarding call. TherapyNotes gives you phone support until 8 pm ET and a 1-hour onboarding session. Both have active Facebook groups, but SimplePractice’s community is larger outside the US.

Step 8: Export guarantee—can you leave with your data?

Read the fine print. SimplePractice retains your data for 90 days after cancellation and exports CSV, PDF, CCD and JSON. TherapyNotes keeps data 60 days and exports CSV, PDF and CCD only. Either way, plan a two-week overlap when you switch so you can migrate without a frantic last-day download.

# Practice Management Software for Group Therapy Practices
As a group practice owner, you understand the complexity of managing multiple therapists, clients, and sessions. One of the most significant challenges you face is finding the right practice management software to streamline your operations, ensuring that you can focus on providing quality care to your clients. In this post, we will explore the essential features and benefits of practice management software specifically designed for group therapy practices, and review two popular options: SimplePractice and TherapyNotes. We will cover the key features, pros, and cons of each software, as well as provide a head-to-head comparison to help you make an informed decision.

## Introduction
Managing a group therapy practice can be overwhelming, especially when it comes to scheduling, billing, and client communication. You need a practice management software that can handle the unique demands of a group practice, including multi-user support, group scheduling, and billing for group sessions. The right software can help you streamline your operations, reduce administrative burdens, and improve client satisfaction. In this post, we will delve into the features and benefits of SimplePractice and TherapyNotes, two popular practice management software options designed for group therapy practices. We will also discuss the key considerations for choosing the best software for your practice, including security, compliance, and scalability.

SimplePractice offers a user-friendly interface and robust customer support, while TherapyNotes provides a comprehensive feature set and excellent reputation among therapists.

## SimplePractice
SimplePractice is a popular practice management software designed for mental health professionals, including group therapy practices. The software offers a range of features that cater to the unique needs of group practices, including:
* Multi-user support: SimplePractice allows multiple users to access the software, making it easy to manage multiple therapists and staff members.
* Group scheduling: The software provides a intuitive scheduling system that enables you to schedule group sessions and manage client appointments.
* Billing and invoicing for group sessions: SimplePractice offers a billing and invoicing system that allows you to generate invoices for group sessions and track payments.

The pros of SimplePractice include its user-friendly interface, robust customer support, and affordable pricing. The software is easy to navigate, and the customer support team is responsive and helpful. However, one of the cons of SimplePractice is its limited customization options, which may not be suitable for practices with unique needs.

SimplePractice is best for group practices that value ease of use and robust customer support. If you're looking for a software that is easy to learn and use, and provides excellent customer support, SimplePractice may be the best option for your practice.

## TherapyNotes
TherapyNotes is another popular practice management software designed for mental health professionals, including group therapy practices. The software offers a range of features that cater to the unique needs of group practices, including:
* Group note templates: TherapyNotes provides pre-built note templates for group sessions, making it easy to document client progress and treatment plans.
* Group scheduling and reminders: The software offers a scheduling system that enables you to schedule group sessions and send reminders to clients and therapists.
* Insurance billing for group therapy sessions: TherapyNotes provides a billing and invoicing system that allows you to generate invoices for group sessions and track payments.

The pros of TherapyNotes include its comprehensive feature set, excellent reputation among therapists, and robust security measures. The software is designed to meet the unique needs of mental health professionals, and its features are tailored to streamline practice management. However, one of the cons of TherapyNotes is its steeper learning curve, which may require more time and effort to master.

TherapyNotes is best for group practices that value a comprehensive feature set and excellent reputation among therapists. If you're looking for a software that provides a wide range of features and is trusted by many therapists, TherapyNotes may be the best option for your practice.

On the other hand, if you prefer a comprehensive feature set and excellent reputation among therapists, TherapyNotes may be the better choice.

When deciding between the two software options, consider the following factors:

# owl practice vs simplepractice canada You’ve opened your practice, hung your shingle, and now you’re staring at two checkout pages: Owl Practice and SimplePractice. Both promise to run your calendar, billing, and notes, but one quotes you in CAD and stores data in Montreal while the other bills USD and keeps servers in Virginia. That difference alone can eat into your revenue and keep you up at night wondering if your college will flag you at audit. In this post I’ll walk you through the exact comparison I wish I’d had when I chose my own EHR—pricing in real dollars, OHIP vs. manual billing, telehealth compliance with CASL/PIPEDA, and whether “unlimited clients” actually matters when you’re capped at 30 sessions a week. **Quick verdict:** Owl Practice wins for Canadian therapists who need provincial billing and Canadian data residency without extra legwork. SimplePractice is the smoother, prettier tool if you’re cash-only or comfortable storing data in the US and handling taxes manually. --- ## Pricing compared for Canadian therapists ### Owl Practice pricing - Solo: $45 CAD/mo (unlimited clients, Canadian support, telehealth included) - Group: $75 CAD/mo for 3 users; each extra clinician +$25 - Yearly discount: 10 % when paid up front ### SimplePractice pricing (USD, add ~35 % FX) - Basic: $39 USD/mo (+$10 if you want telehealth) - Pro: $59 USD/mo (includes Wiley notes & telehealth) - Each extra clinician: +$15 USD/mo - Yearly discount: 15 % **Bottom line:** After exchange, SimplePractice Basic with telehealth lands around $78 CAD—$33 more than Owl—and you still don’t get provincial billing codes. --- ## Canadian billing & provincial health plans ### Owl Practice - Pre-loaded fee codes for OHIP, MSP, AHCIP, MCP, NIHB - HST/GST auto-added to invoices - Direct electronic submission to Telus Health, ClaimSecure, NIHB - OHIP e-claim beta launching Q4 2024 ### SimplePractice - Lets you create custom fee schedules, but you type in every provincial code manually - Exports CMS-1500 PDFs; no e-remittance to provincial portals - You reconcile payments outside the system If even 20 % of your revenue is from provincial plans, the hours you’ll spend reconciling explanations of benefits make Owl cheaper in practice. --- ## Telehealth: CASL & PIPEDA compliance Owl’s video servers sit in AWS ca-central-1; no data crosses the border. Client reminders use Canadian SMS gateways, so you’re automatically CASL-compliant. SimplePractice video routes through US servers. Most colleges allow it, but you’ll need to add a consent clause that data leaves Canada and keep a copy on file. --- ## Data residency: where is Canadian client data stored? | Feature | Owl Practice | SimplePractice | |---------|--------------|---------------| | Server location | Montreal (ca-central-1) | N. Virginia (us-east-1) | | Backups | Same region | US | | College flag risk | Low | Medium—check your standards | Many Canadian colleges “recommend” Canadian data residency; some require it. If you’re audited, Owl gives you one less headache. --- ## Ease of use & onboarding Owl’s setup wizard asks for “province,” “postal code,” and loads Canadian holidays. French-language intake forms are two clicks away. SimplePractice has the slicker UI and faster mobile app, but the default dropdown still shows “State” and “Zip” unless you manually toggle country—every form, every time. --- ## Integrations & add-ons Owl keeps it Canadian: Telus e-claims, Stripe Canada, Fullscript CA, Mailchimp, Google/Outlook calendars. SimplePractice offers 30+ integrations—Wiley planners, Headway, BetterHelp—but most are US-centric. If you cross-refer to US providers or want the Wiley notes, that’s a win for SimplePractice. --- ## Customer support hours in Canadian time zones - **Owl:** Live chat & phone Mon-Fri 8 am-8 pm EST; French reps available - **SimplePractice:** Email & chat 6 am-5 pm PST (9 am-8 pm EST); no French support If you’re in Atlantic time, Owl’s 8 pm EST line still answers; SimplePractice’s chat is offline. --- ## User reviews from Canadian therapists - **Owl:** 4.7/5 on the CCPA directory (n=312). Users love the OHIP batch upload; main gripe is dated interface. - **SimplePractice:** 4.5/5 on Capterra CA (n=1,100). Praised for UX; complaints about USD pricing and no Canadian billing. --- ## Pros & cons table | Aspect | Owl Practice Pros | Owl Practice Cons | SimplePractice Pros | SimplePractice Cons | |--------|-----------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------| | Data | Canadian servers | UI looks 2015 | Modern UX | US servers | | Billing | Provincial codes built-in | Fewer integrations | Tons of US add-ons | Manual tax setup | | Price | CAD, no FX | Mobile app basic | Polished mobile | USD + 35 % | | Language | Bilingual forms | Canada-only clients | Frequent updates | No French support | | Taxes | T4A auto | — | — | HST/GST manual | --- ## Who should choose which **Choose Owl Practice if you:** - Bill provincial health plans or NIHB - Need CRA-compliant receipts/T4A slips - Prefer Canadian data residency out of the box - Want CAD pricing certainty **Choose SimplePractice if you:** - Run a cash-only or US-cross-border practice - Value slick mobile apps and Wiley note templates - Don’t mind storing data in the US and handling HST manually - Need integrations like Headway or BetterHelp --- ## FAQ ### Is Owl Practice PIPEDA compliant for Canadian therapists? Yes—servers and backups stay in AWS ca-central-1 (Montreal), so you meet PIPEDA and most provincial privacy acts without extra paperwork. ### Can SimplePractice submit OHIP e-claims? Not at present. You can print CMS-1500 forms or manually enter claims in the MOH portal; reconciliation is done outside SimplePractice. ### Does Owl Practice generate T4A slips for CRA? It auto-exports a T4A data file at year-end; you upload it to CRA’s online portal—no spreadsheet gymnastics. ### Which platform is cheaper after USD exchange? At 1.35 FX, SimplePractice Basic with telehealth costs roughly $78 CAD vs. Owl’s $45 CAD solo plan. ### Do either integrate with Quebec’s RAMQ? Neither platform offers direct RAMQ submission yet; Owl plans to add it in 2025. --- ## Conclusion If you’re billing provincial plans or simply want Canadian data residency and tax compliance without manual hacks, Owl Practice is the safer, cheaper pick. If you’re cash-only and live inside your phone, SimplePractice’s polished UX and deep US integrations are tempting—just budget for FX fees and an extra consent form. Tax Deductions for Therapists in Private Practice: A Software Guide

# Tax Deductions for Therapists in Private Practice: A Software Guide If you’ve ever stared at a shoebox of receipts on April 14th, wondering which ones the IRS will actually accept, you’re not alone. Most of us became therapists to help people, not to become part-time tax accountants. Yet every year we scramble to remember which software subscriptions, mileage logs, and continuing-education receipts count as legitimate tax deductions for our private practice. This post walks you through the biggest write-off categories that apply to self-employed therapists in the US, then shows you exactly how to track them in QuickBooks and Wave so next tax season feels like a five-minute task instead of a five-day panic. > **Quick verdict:** > QuickBooks gives you the deepest mileage and integration tools if you don’t mind the monthly fee; Wave keeps every core deduction-tracking feature free, making it the cheapest way to stay audit-ready. ## Office Space: Home-Office & Rent Deductions The IRS lets you deduct the square footage you use *exclusively* for therapy, whether that’s a spare bedroom turned into a telehealth studio or a rented suite you see clients in twice a week. You can choose the simplified method ($5 per sq ft up to 300 sq ft) or the actual-expense method (percentage of rent, utilities, repairs). Either way, you need contemporaneous records—photos, floor-plan sketches, and utility bills—stored in one place so you’re not hunting through email if you get a letter from the IRS. **QuickBooks approach:** Create a category called “Home-Office” under Expenses. Upload photos of the room and the square-footage worksheet as attachments to a $0 transaction; the app keeps them time-stamped. If you use actual expenses, link your utility bank feed; QuickBooks auto-suggests splits based on the percentage you set once. Run a “Home-Office” report in two clicks at year-end. **Wave approach:** Wave is free, so you can create a custom category “Home-Office – Simplified” or “Home-Office – Actual.” Scan utility bills with the mobile receipt tool; the OCR reads the amount and date. Because Wave doesn’t have a built-in square-footage calculator, keep a simple Google Sheet and upload it as a PDF so everything is in one account. **Best for:** - QuickBooks if you want automatic percentage splits and plan to claim actual expenses. - Wave if you’re comfortable with a spreadsheet and want zero monthly cost. ## Software & Tech: Practice-Management Subscriptions, Telehealth, Security Your EHR, HIPAA-compliant video platform, encrypted email add-ons, and even your password-manager subscription are deductible. The key is separating business vs. personal use. If you pay annually, capture that large hit in the month it occurs; if you pay monthly, the software should auto-import. **QuickBooks:** Set up a recurring “Software & Tech” rule. Any charge from SimplePractice, TherapyNotes, VSee, etc., auto-funnels into the category. If you buy an annual plan, tag it as an asset and let QuickBooks auto-expense it over 12 months so your profit-and-loss looks smooth. **Wave:** Wave itself is free, so you can create a sub-category called “Annual Subscriptions” and manually enter the prepaid amount. Attach the invoice PDF so you have proof of the business purpose. Wave’s bank-import will still pull the charge; you just recategorize it. **Best for:** - QuickBooks if you want automated amortization and rules that learn your vendors. - Wave if you prefer to handle amortization manually and want $0 overhead. ## Education & Training: CEUs, Journals, License Renewals You can deduct the cost of CE courses, journal subscriptions, and state license renewals. Travel to a conference? That’s under “Travel,” but the course fee itself sits here. Keep certificates of completion and itemized receipts. **QuickBooks:** Use the “Education & Training” category. If you travel for a workshop, split the transaction: fee here, mileage/travel under “Travel.” Attach the CE certificate PDF so an auditor sees the business connection instantly. **Wave:** Create the same category and scan receipts from PESI, NASW, or your state board. Wave’s mobile scanner lets you add a note (“40 CE ethics hours required for LICSW renewal”) so you don’t forget why the expense exists. **Best for:** Either works; QuickBooks’ rules save time if you attend multiple trainings yearly. ## Business Insurance: Malpractice, Health, Liability Premiums for malpractice, general liability, and even a percentage of your self-employed health insurance can be deductible. Health-insurance deductions go on page 1 of Form 1040, but you still need proof of payment. **QuickBooks:** Set up “Insurance – Malpractice,” “Insurance – Health,” etc. Link the insurance bank draft; rules auto-categorize. At year-end, run a vendor report to show total paid to each carrier. **Wave:** Create the same categories. Because Wave doesn’t have vendor reports as robust as QuickBooks, export the CSV and pivot in Excel. Still free, just an extra five minutes. **Best for:** QuickBooks if you want one-click vendor totals; Wave if you’re comfortable with spreadsheets. ## Travel & Commuting: Mileage, Client Visits, Conferences Any trip from your office to a client’s home, a hospital, or a conference hotel is deductible. Your daily commute from home to your office is *not*. The 2024 IRS rate is 67¢ per mile; you need a log with date, purpose, starting/ending odometer. **QuickBooks:** Built-in mileage tracker uses your phone’s GPS. Swipe right for business, left for personal. At year-end, export the standard-mileage report; QuickBooks multiplies by the IRS rate. **Wave:** Wave itself has no GPS tracker, but you can integrate MileIQ (also free for 40 drives/mo). Export the MileIQ CSV and import as a journal entry into Wave under “Travel – Mileage.” **Best for:** QuickBooks if you want an all-in-one GPS log. Wave if you’re okay using a separate mileage app and importing. ## Head-to-Head Comparison | Feature | QuickBooks | Wave | |---|---|---| | Monthly cost | $30–$90 depending on plan | $0 core accounting | | Mileage tracking | Built-in GPS | Requires third-party app | | Receipt scanning | Included on all plans | Free mobile scan | | Amortize prepaid software | Automatic | Manual | | Vendor expense report | One-click | Export to Excel | | Bank-feed rules | Advanced AI | Basic | | Audit attachment storage | Unlimited | Unlimited | | Health-insurance category | Pre-built | Create custom | ## Who Should Choose Which **Pick QuickBooks if:** - You drive 50+ miles a week for clients and want automatic mileage logs. - You hate spreadsheets and will pay for automation. - You have employees and need payroll tax deduction tracking. **Pick Wave if:** - You work from one location and mileage is minimal. - You love free and don’t mind manual amortization. - You just need clean categories and scanned receipts. ## FAQ ### What tax deductions are available for therapists in private practice? You can deduct office rent or a home-office percentage, software subscriptions (EHR, telehealth), continuing-education fees, malpractice and health-insurance premiums, mileage for client visits, supplies, and a portion of your phone and internet. Keep receipts and logs in accounting software so the numbers are audit-ready. ### How can accounting software like QuickBooks and Wave help track therapist tax deductions? Both let you create custom categories matching IRS deduction types—office, software, travel, insurance. QuickBooks adds GPS mileage and auto-rules; Wave gives free receipt scanning and invoicing. Either one produces a year-end “tax-deductible expenses” report you can hand to your CPA or import into tax software. ### What expenses qualify as tax-deductible for self-employed therapists? Ordinary and necessary costs for running your practice: office space, software, CEUs, license renewals, malpractice insurance, business liability, mileage between offices or to client homes, office supplies, and marketing. Personal therapy, daily commuting, and non-CE books do not qualify. ### Can therapists deduct home-office expenses using tax software? Yes, if the space is used regularly and exclusively for therapy. You can claim $5 per square foot up to 300 sq ft (simplified) or the actual percentage of rent, utilities, and repairs. QuickBooks and Wave both let you create a “Home-Office” category and attach floor-plan PDFs so you have documentation. ### How do I organize receipts for tax deductions with practice-management tools? Use Wave’s free mobile scanner or QuickBooks’ built-in tool: snap a photo immediately after purchase, tag the category (e.g., “Software & Tech”), and add a short note (“SimplePractice annual HIPAA plan”). Store digital copies in the software; discard the paper if you back up your cloud files. ## Conclusion & Next Steps You became a therapist to change lives, not to chase receipts. Claiming every legitimate deduction means more money to invest in training, better software, or simply taking a breath. Pick QuickBooks if you want the smoothest mileage and automation experience, or Wave if free and manual works for you.
